how-many-megapixels-for-large-print.question: How many megapixels do I need for a large print?
how-many-megapixels-for-large-print.answer: Depends on DPI and viewing distance. At arm's-length viewing, 300 DPI is the gold standard — a 24 MP photo prints sharply at ~20×13 in. At 6-foot viewing distance, you can drop to ~60 PPI and a 24 MP photo covers a 60-inch-wide wall print. Use this tool to experiment with both.

why-phone-48mp-shows-12mp.question: Why does my phone advertise 48 MP but photos show as 12 MP?
why-phone-48mp-shows-12mp.answer: Pixel binning: phones combine four adjacent sensor pixels into one output pixel, improving low-light performance and reducing noise. You can usually access the advertised 48 MP via a 'high resolution' or 'RAW' mode, at the cost of noisier shots in dim light.

difference-300-dpi-vs-240-dpi.question: What's the difference between 300 DPI and 240 DPI for photo prints?
difference-300-dpi-vs-240-dpi.answer: For prints viewed at arm's length, 300 DPI is the traditional sharp threshold and 240 DPI is the 'good enough' threshold — the difference is only visible on close inspection. For prints viewed from farther away (framed art on a wall), 240 DPI is indistinguishable from 300 DPI to most viewers.
